mn44:0.2: The Shorter Series of Questions and Answers
mn44:1.2: at one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground.
mn44:1.3: Then Visākha the lay follower went to the *bhikkhuni* Dhammadinnā; having gone, he bowed to the *bhikkhuni* Dhammadinnā and sat to one side. Sitting to one side, Visākha the lay follower said this to the *bhikkhuni* Dhammadinnā:
mn44:10.1: “But, lady, is the noble eightfold path *saṅkhata* or *asaṅkhata*?”
mn44:10.2: “Friend Visākha, the noble eightfold path is *saṅkhata*.”
mn44:11.1: “Lady, are the three aggregates included by the noble eightfold path, or is the noble eightfold path included by the three aggregates?”
mn44:11.2: “Friend Visākha, the three aggregates are not included by the noble eightfold path; rather, friend Visākha, the noble eightfold path is included by the three aggregates.
mn44:11.3: Friend Visākha, right speech, right action, and right livelihood: these *dhammas* are included in the aggregate of conduct.
mn44:11.4: Right effort, right *sati*, and right *samādhi*: these *dhammas* are included in the aggregate of *samādhi*.
mn44:11.5: Right view and right intention: these *dhammas* are included in the aggregate of discernment [*paññā*].”
mn44:12.1: “But what, lady, is *samādhi*, what *dhammas* are the *nimitta* of *samādhi*, what *dhammas* are the supports of *samādhi*, and what is the development of *samādhi* [*samādhibhāvanā*]?”
mn44:12.2: “Singleness of mind [*cittassa ekaggatā*], friend Visākha: this is *samādhi*.
mn44:12.4: The four right strivings are the supports of *samādhi*.
mn44:12.5: The repetition, development [*bhāvanā*], and frequent practice of those very *dhammas*: this is the development of *samādhi* [*samādhibhāvanā*] here.”
mn44:13.3: the bodily *saṅkhāra*, the verbal *saṅkhāra*, and the mental *saṅkhāra* [*cittasaṅkhāro*].”
mn44:14.1: “But what, lady, is the bodily *saṅkhāra*, what is the verbal *saṅkhāra*, and what is the mental *saṅkhāra* [*cittasaṅkhāro*]?”
mn44:14.2: “In-breaths and out-breaths, friend Visākha, are the bodily *saṅkhāra*; thoughts and examinations [*vitakkavicārā*] are the verbal *saṅkhāra*; and *saññā* and *vedanā* are the mental *saṅkhāra* [*cittasaṅkhāro*].”
mn44:15.1: “But why, lady, are in-breaths and out-breaths the bodily *saṅkhāra*? Why are thoughts and examinations [*vitakkavicārā*] the verbal *saṅkhāra*? Why are *saññā* and *vedanā* the mental *saṅkhāra* [*cittasaṅkhāro*]?”
mn44:15.2: “In-breaths and out-breaths, friend Visākha, are bodily; these *dhammas* are bound up with the body. Therefore in-breaths and out-breaths are the bodily *saṅkhāra*.
mn44:15.3: Friend Visākha, first one has thought [*vitakketvā*] and examined [*vicāretvā*], then afterward one breaks into speech. Therefore thoughts and examinations [*vitakkavicārā*] are the verbal *saṅkhāra*.
mn44:15.4: *Saññā* and *vedanā*, friend Visākha, are mental; these *dhammas* are bound up with mind [*citta*]. Therefore *saññā* and *vedanā* are the mental *saṅkhāra* [*cittasaṅkhāro*].”
mn44:16.1: “But how, lady, does the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and what is felt occur?”
mn44:16.2: “Friend Visākha, it does not occur to a *bhikkhu* who is attaining the cessation of *saññā* and what is felt:
mn44:16.3: ‘I will attain the cessation of *saññā* and what is felt,’ or ‘I am attaining the cessation of *saññā* and what is felt,’ or ‘I have attained the cessation of *saññā* and what is felt.’
mn44:16.4: Rather, beforehand his mind [*citta*] has been developed [*bhāvitaṁ*] in such a way that it leads him to that state.”
mn44:17.1: “But, lady, when a *bhikkhu* is attaining the cessation of *saññā* and what is felt, which *dhammas* cease first: the bodily *saṅkhāra*, or the verbal *saṅkhāra*, or the mental *saṅkhāra* [*cittasaṅkhāro*]?”
mn44:17.2: “Friend Visākha, when a *bhikkhu* is attaining the cessation of *saññā* and what is felt, the verbal *saṅkhāra* ceases first, then the bodily *saṅkhāra*, then the mental *saṅkhāra* [*cittasaṅkhāro*].”
mn44:18.1: “But how, lady, does emergence from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and what is felt occur?”
mn44:18.2: “Friend Visākha, it does not occur to a *bhikkhu* who is emerging from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and what is felt:
mn44:18.3: ‘I will emerge from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and what is felt,’ or ‘I am emerging from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and what is felt,’ or ‘I have emerged from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and what is felt.’
mn44:18.4: Rather, beforehand his mind [*citta*] has been developed [*bhāvitaṁ*] in such a way that it leads him to that state.”
mn44:19.1: “But, lady, when a *bhikkhu* is emerging from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and what is felt, which *dhammas* arise first: the bodily *saṅkhāra*, or the verbal *saṅkhāra*, or the mental *saṅkhāra* [*cittasaṅkhāro*]?”
mn44:19.2: “Friend Visākha, when a *bhikkhu* is emerging from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and what is felt, the mental *saṅkhāra* [*cittasaṅkhāro*] arises first, then the bodily *saṅkhāra*, then the verbal *saṅkhāra*.”
mn44:2.2: What, lady, has been called identity by the *Bhagavā*?”
mn44:2.3: “These five upādāna aggregates, friend Visākha, have been called identity by the *Bhagavā*:
mn44:2.4: that is, the upādāna aggregate of form [*rūpa*], the upādāna aggregate of *vedanā*, the upādāna aggregate of *saññā*, the upādāna aggregate of *saṅkhāras*, and the upādāna aggregate of *viññāṇa*.
mn44:2.5: These five upādāna aggregates, friend Visākha, have been called identity by the *Bhagavā*.”
mn44:2.6: Saying, “*Sādhu*, lady,” Visākha the lay follower rejoiced in and approved of what the *bhikkhuni* Dhammadinnā had said, and asked her a further question:
mn44:20.1: “But, lady, when a *bhikkhu* has emerged from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and what is felt, how many contacts touch him?”
mn44:20.2: “Friend Visākha, when a *bhikkhu* has emerged from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and what is felt, three contacts touch him: the empty contact, the *nimitta*less contact, and the undirected contact.”
mn44:21.1: “But, lady, when a *bhikkhu* has emerged from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and what is felt, toward what does his mind [*citta*] incline, slope, and lean?”
mn44:21.2: “Friend Visākha, when a *bhikkhu* has emerged from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and what is felt, his mind [*citta*] inclines to seclusion [*viveka*], slopes to seclusion [*viveka*], and leans to seclusion [*viveka*].”
mn44:23.1: “But what, lady, is pleasant *vedanā*, what is painful *vedanā*, and what is neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*?”
mn44:23.2: “Whatever bodily or mental *sukha*, agreeable experience, there is, friend Visākha:
mn44:23.4: Whatever bodily or mental *dukkha*, disagreeable experience, there is, friend Visākha:
mn44:23.6: Whatever bodily or mental experience there is that is neither agreeable nor disagreeable, friend Visākha:
mn44:24.1: “But, lady, in what way is pleasant *vedanā* *sukha* and in what way *dukkha*? In what way is painful *vedanā* *sukha* and in what way *dukkha*? In what way is neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā* *sukha* and in what way *dukkha*?”
mn44:24.2: “Pleasant *vedanā*, friend Visākha, is *sukha* when it remains and *dukkha* when it changes.
mn44:24.3: Painful *vedanā* is *dukkha* when it remains and *sukha* when it changes.
mn44:24.4: Neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā* is *sukha* when there is knowledge, and *dukkha* when there is non-knowledge.”
mn44:25.1: “But, lady, what underlying tendency lies underlying pleasant *vedanā*? What underlying tendency lies underlying painful *vedanā*? What underlying tendency lies underlying neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*?”
mn44:25.2: “The underlying tendency to passion lies underlying pleasant *vedanā*, friend Visākha; the underlying tendency to aversion lies underlying painful *vedanā*; the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] lies underlying neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*.”
mn44:26.1: “Lady, does the underlying tendency to passion lie underlying all pleasant *vedanā*? Does the underlying tendency to aversion lie underlying all painful *vedanā*? Does the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] lie underlying all neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*?”
mn44:26.2: “No, friend Visākha, the underlying tendency to passion does not lie underlying all pleasant *vedanā*; the underlying tendency to aversion does not lie underlying all painful *vedanā*; the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] does not lie underlying all neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*.”
mn44:27.1: “But, lady, what should be abandoned in pleasant *vedanā*? What should be abandoned in painful *vedanā*? What should be abandoned in neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*?”
mn44:27.2: “The underlying tendency to passion should be abandoned in pleasant *vedanā*, friend Visākha; the underlying tendency to aversion should be abandoned in painful *vedanā*; the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] should be abandoned in neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*.”
mn44:28.1: “Lady, should the underlying tendency to passion be abandoned in all pleasant *vedanā*? Should the underlying tendency to aversion be abandoned in all painful *vedanā*? Should the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] be abandoned in all neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*?”
mn44:28.10: With that he abandons blindness [*avijjā*]; the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] does not lie underlying there.”
mn44:28.2: “No, friend Visākha, the underlying tendency to passion should not be abandoned in all pleasant *vedanā*; the underlying tendency to aversion should not be abandoned in all painful *vedanā*; the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] should not be abandoned in all neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*.
mn44:28.3: Here, friend Visākha, a *bhikkhu*, quite secluded [*vivicceva*] from sensual pleasures, secluded [*vivicca*] from unwholesome *dhammas*, enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*, with thought [*savitakkaṁ*] and examination [*savicāraṁ*], born of seclusion [*vivekajaṁ*], with *pīti* and *sukha*.
mn44:28.4: With that he abandons passion; the underlying tendency to passion does not lie underlying there.
mn44:28.5: Here, friend Visākha, a *bhikkhu* reflects thus:
mn44:28.6: ‘Oh, when will I enter and dwell in that dimension [*āyatanaṁ*] in which the noble ones now enter and dwell?’
mn44:28.7: As one sets up longing for the unsurpassed liberations, distress arises conditioned by longing.
mn44:28.8: With that he abandons aversion; the underlying tendency to aversion does not lie underlying there.
mn44:28.9: Here, friend Visākha, with the abandoning of *sukha* and the abandoning of *dukkha*, and with the previous disappearance of gladness and distress, a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*, which is neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*, with purification of *sati* by equanimity [*upekkhā*].
mn44:29.1: “But, lady, what is the counterpart of pleasant *vedanā*?”
mn44:29.10: “Liberation, friend Visākha, is the counterpart of knowledge.”
mn44:29.11: “But, lady, what is the counterpart of liberation?”
mn44:29.12: “*Nibbāna*, friend Visākha, is the counterpart of liberation.”
mn44:29.13: “But, lady, what is the counterpart of *nibbāna*?”
mn44:29.14: “You have gone too far with the question, friend Visākha; you were not able to grasp the limit of the questions.
mn44:29.15: For the holy life, friend Visākha, plunges into *nibbāna*, has *nibbāna* as its destination, and has *nibbāna* as its culmination.
mn44:29.16: If you wish, friend Visākha, you may go to the *Bhagavā* and ask him about this matter; and as the *Bhagavā* explains it to you, so you should remember it.”
mn44:29.2: “Painful *vedanā*, friend Visākha, is the counterpart of pleasant *vedanā*.”
mn44:29.3: “But, lady, what is the counterpart of painful *vedanā*?”
mn44:29.4: “Pleasant *vedanā*, friend Visākha, is the counterpart of painful *vedanā*.”
mn44:29.5: “But, lady, what is the counterpart of neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*?”
mn44:29.6: “Blindness [*avijjā*], friend Visākha, is the counterpart of neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*.”
mn44:29.7: “But, lady, what is the counterpart of blindness [*avijjā*]?”
mn44:29.8: “Knowledge, friend Visākha, is the counterpart of blindness [*avijjā*].”
mn44:29.9: “But, lady, what is the counterpart of knowledge?”
mn44:3.1: “‘The arising of identity, the arising of identity,’ lady, is said.
mn44:3.2: What, lady, has been called the arising of identity by the *Bhagavā*?”
mn44:3.3: “It is this *taṇhā*, friend Visākha, that leads to renewed existence, accompanied by delight and passion, delighting here and there, that is:
mn44:3.4: *taṇhā* for sensual pleasures, *taṇhā* for *bhava*, and *taṇhā* for non-*bhava*.
mn44:3.5: This, friend Visākha, has been called the arising of identity by the *Bhagavā*.”
mn44:30.1: Then Visākha the lay follower rejoiced in and approved of what the *bhikkhuni* Dhammadinnā had said, rose from his seat, bowed to the *bhikkhuni* Dhammadinnā, circumambulated her keeping her to his right, and went to the *Bhagavā*. Having gone, he bowed to the *Bhagavā* and sat to one side.
mn44:30.2: Sitting to one side, Visākha the lay follower reported to the *Bhagavā* the entire discussion he had had with the *bhikkhuni* Dhammadinnā.
mn44:30.3: When this had been said, the *Bhagavā* said this to Visākha the lay follower:
mn44:30.4: “The *bhikkhuni* Dhammadinnā is wise, Visākha; the *bhikkhuni* Dhammadinnā has great discernment [*paññā*], Visākha.
mn44:30.5: If you had asked me this matter, Visākha, I too would have explained it in exactly the same way as the *bhikkhuni* Dhammadinnā explained it.
mn44:30.6: This is precisely its meaning. And so you should remember it.”
mn44:30.8: Satisfied, Visākha the lay follower rejoiced in what the *Bhagavā* had said.
mn44:30.9: The Shorter Series of Questions and Answers is finished, the fourth.
mn44:4.1: “‘The cessation of identity, the cessation of identity,’ lady, is said.
mn44:4.2: What, lady, has been called the cessation of identity by the *Bhagavā*?”
mn44:4.3: “It is the remainderless fading away and cessation of that very *taṇhā*, its giving up, relinquishment, release, and non-attachment.
mn44:4.4: This, friend Visākha, has been called the cessation of identity by the *Bhagavā*.”
mn44:5.1: “‘The practice leading to the cessation of identity, the practice leading to the cessation of identity,’ lady, is said.
mn44:5.2: What, lady, has been called the practice leading to the cessation of identity by the *Bhagavā*?”
mn44:5.3: “This very noble eightfold path, friend Visākha, has been called the practice leading to the cessation of identity by the *Bhagavā*, that is:
mn44:5.4: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right *sati*, and right *samādhi*.”
mn44:6.1: “Lady, is that very upādāna the five upādāna aggregates, or is upādāna apart from the five upādāna aggregates?”
mn44:6.2: “Friend Visākha, that very upādāna is not the five upādāna aggregates, nor is upādāna apart from the five upādāna aggregates.
mn44:6.3: The desire and passion for the five upādāna aggregates, friend Visākha: that is the upādāna there.”
mn44:7.1: “But how, lady, does identity view arise?”
mn44:7.2: “Here, friend Visākha, an unlearned ordinary person, who has not seen the noble ones, is unskilled in the *dhamma* of the noble ones and undisciplined in the *dhamma* of the noble ones; who has not seen true persons, is unskilled in the *dhamma* of true persons and undisciplined in the *dhamma* of true persons,
mn44:7.3: regards form [*rūpa*] as self, or self as possessing form [*rūpa*], or form [*rūpa*] as in self, or self as in form [*rūpa*].
mn44:7.7: regards *viññāṇa* as self, or self as possessing *viññāṇa*, or *viññāṇa* as in self, or self as in *viññāṇa*.
mn44:7.8: In this way, friend Visākha, identity view arises.”
mn44:8.1: “But how, lady, does identity view not arise?”
mn44:8.2: “Here, friend Visākha, a learned noble disciple, who has seen the noble ones, is skilled in the *dhamma* of the noble ones and well disciplined in the *dhamma* of the noble ones; who has seen true persons, is skilled in the *dhamma* of true persons and well disciplined in the *dhamma* of true persons,
mn44:8.3: does not regard form [*rūpa*] as self, or self as possessing form [*rūpa*], or form [*rūpa*] as in self, or self as in form [*rūpa*].
mn44:8.7: does not regard *viññāṇa* as self, or self as possessing *viññāṇa*, or *viññāṇa* as in self, or self as in *viññāṇa*.
mn44:8.8: In this way, friend Visākha, identity view does not arise.”
mn44:9.1: “But what, lady, is the noble eightfold path?”
mn44:9.2: “This very noble eightfold path, friend Visākha, that is:
mn44:9.3: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right *sati*, and right *samādhi*.”
